content.text: Why Hands-On Labs Are Necessary, Not Optional

Declarative knowledge -- understanding that hallucination occurs, knowing the validation checklist exists -- does not transfer reliably into the procedural skill of actually detecting errors under working conditions. Studies of skills training in high-stakes professional contexts consistently show that knowledge-based assessments overpredict practical performance. Employees who score 90% on a quiz about AI validation frequently miss planted errors in lab simulations, because quiz performance and detection performance involve different cognitive processes.

A certification that requires only a quiz is a credential that certifies someone can recall policy, not that they can use the tool safely. For roles where an undetected AI error causes client harm, regulatory exposure, or financial loss, that distinction matters.
Three Certification Tracks

attrs.alt: GenAI Enterprise Certification Paths -- three tracks from User Proficiency to Builder/Engineer

content.text: The enterprise certification programme defines three tracks, each culminating in a time-limited credential. Tracks are not hierarchical -- a Track A user certification is not a prerequisite for Track B. They are parallel paths based on how the role interacts with AI outputs, not seniority or technical depth.
Track
Name
Target Roles
Total Hours
Validity
Renewal Trigger
A
AI User Proficiency
End users who receive or act on AI outputs indirectly
6h (2h foundation + 4h tool practitioner)
18 months
Tool version change or curriculum update
B
AI Knowledge Worker
Professionals who validate and use AI outputs directly in their work product
12h (6h required + 4h optional advanced)
12 months
Annual lab re-sit or curriculum update
C
AI Builder / Engineer
Developers, data scientists, MLOps engineers who build or configure AI systems
23h+ across foundation, labs, red-team
12 months
Annual project reassessment
Designing Effective Lab Scenarios

Lab scenarios must be realistic enough that learners cannot distinguish them from real work, difficult enough that passing requires genuine skill, and calibrated enough that planted errors are discoverable through the taught validation process -- not through obscure knowledge the curriculum did not cover.
Lab Scenario Requirements
Realistic inputs.

Use anonymised or synthetic versions of actual documents from the deployment domain. A legal analyst doing a contract extraction lab should work on a document that resembles the contracts they process, not a simplified example.
Planted errors at realistic severity.

Include errors that a model would plausibly produce (numeric transpositions, jurisdiction substitution, near-miss dates) not obviously absurd errors. The goal is to test detection skill, not to trick.
Time pressure that reflects real conditions.
A validation task that takes 20 minutes in a lab but must be done in 8 minutes in production is not measuring the right capability. Set lab time limits that approximate the expected workflow time.
Documentation requirement.
Require learners to document their findings with source references. This tests not just whether they spotted the error but whether they can trace it, which is the skill needed in practice.

import os
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional


@dataclass
class LabScenario:
    scenario_id: str
    title: str
    track: str          # "user", "knowledge_worker", "builder"
    duration_minutes: int
    setup_instructions: str
    task_description: str
    planted_errors: list[str]   # errors the learner must detect
    scoring_rubric: dict[str, int]   # criterion -> max points

    def max_score(self) -> int:
        return sum(self.scoring_rubric.values())

    def pass_threshold(self) -> int:
        return int(self.max_score() * 0.75)


@dataclass
class LabAttempt:
    scenario_id: str
    learner_id: str
    errors_detected: list[str]
    errors_missed: list[str]
    false_positives: list[str]   # flagged as error but were correct
    time_taken_minutes: int
    notes: str = ""

    def score(self, rubric: dict[str, int], planted: list[str]) -> int:
        total = 0
        for error in self.errors_detected:
            if error in planted:
                total += rubric.get("error_detection", 10)
        for fp in self.false_positives:
            total -= rubric.get("false_positive_penalty", 3)
        if self.notes and len(self.notes) > 20:
            total += rubric.get("documentation_quality", 5)
        return max(0, total)

    def feedback(self, planted: list[str]) -> list[str]:
        messages = []
        for missed in self.errors_missed:
            messages.append(f"Missed: {missed}")
        for fp in self.false_positives:
            messages.append(f"False positive (correct output flagged incorrectly): {fp}")
        if not self.errors_missed and not self.false_positives:
            messages.append("All planted errors detected with no false positives")
        return messages


# Lab scenario: hallucination detection in contract extraction
contract_lab = LabScenario(
    scenario_id="LAB-LEGAL-P2-01",
    title="Contract Liability Extraction -- Hallucination Detection",
    track="knowledge_worker",
    duration_minutes=45,
    setup_instructions=(
        "You will review the AI-generated extraction report for a sample supply agreement. "
        "The extraction was produced by ContractSummariser v2.4 on a 68-page contract "
        "with two nested schedule appendices. Your task is to validate the extraction "
        "against the source document using the standard validation checklist."
    ),
    task_description=(
        "Using the validation checklist, review the AI extraction output and identify "
        "any errors, hallucinations, or omissions. Document each finding with a "
        "reference to the source page and clause number. Submit your findings via "
        "the lab assessment form."
    ),
    planted_errors=[
        "Liability cap stated as GBP 500,000 -- source document Schedule 2 clause 4.1 states GBP 50,000",
        "Governing law stated as 'English and Welsh law' -- source states 'Scottish law' (clause 18)",
        "Termination notice period stated as '30 days' -- source Schedule 1 clause 7 states '90 days'",
    ],
    scoring_rubric={
        "error_detection": 15,      # per detected planted error
        "false_positive_penalty": 5,  # per incorrectly flagged correct item
        "documentation_quality": 10,  # evidence references provided
    },
)

# Simulate two learner attempts
attempt_good = LabAttempt(
    scenario_id="LAB-LEGAL-P2-01",
    learner_id="EMP-003",
    errors_detected=[
        "Liability cap stated as GBP 500,000 -- source document Schedule 2 clause 4.1 states GBP 50,000",
        "Governing law stated as 'English and Welsh law' -- source states 'Scottish law' (clause 18)",
        "Termination notice period stated as '30 days' -- source Schedule 1 clause 7 states '90 days'",
    ],
    errors_missed=[],
    false_positives=[],
    time_taken_minutes=38,
    notes="All three figures verified against Schedule 2 and Schedule 1 before flagging.",
)

attempt_weak = LabAttempt(
    scenario_id="LAB-LEGAL-P2-01",
    learner_id="EMP-007",
    errors_detected=[
        "Liability cap stated as GBP 500,000 -- source document Schedule 2 clause 4.1 states GBP 50,000",
    ],
    errors_missed=[
        "Governing law stated as 'English and Welsh law' -- source states 'Scottish law' (clause 18)",
        "Termination notice period stated as '30 days' -- source Schedule 1 clause 7 states '90 days'",
    ],
    false_positives=["Payment terms -- these were actually correct in the extraction"],
    time_taken_minutes=41,
    notes="",
)

for attempt in [attempt_good, attempt_weak]:
    score = attempt.score(contract_lab.scoring_rubric, contract_lab.planted_errors)
    threshold = contract_lab.pass_threshold()
    result = "PASS" if score >= threshold else "FAIL -- remediation required"
    print(f"{attempt.learner_id}: {score}/{contract_lab.max_score()} -- {result}")
    for fb in attempt.feedback(contract_lab.planted_errors):
        print(f"  {fb}")
    print()
Certification Lifecycle Management

Certifications have expiry dates for substantive reasons, not administrative ones. A certification issued when the tool was version 2.1 may not reflect competence with version 3.0. An employee certified before a significant policy change may be operating under outdated assumptions. The certification registry must track expiry, trigger renewal communications, and block tool access for expired certificates in roles where that is appropriate.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date, timedelta
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional


class CertificationStatus(Enum):
    NOT_CERTIFIED = "not_certified"
    CERTIFIED = "certified"
    EXPIRED = "expired"
    SUSPENDED = "suspended"   # incident or policy violation


@dataclass
class Certification:
    cert_id: str
    cert_name: str
    track: str
    issued_date: date
    validity_months: int
    renewal_requirements: list[str]
    issuing_organisation: str   # internal or external
    holder_id: str
    suspended: bool = False
    suspension_reason: Optional[str] = None

    @property
    def expiry_date(self) -> date:
        # Approximate: add months
        m = self.issued_date.month - 1 + self.validity_months
        year = self.issued_date.year + m // 12
        month = m % 12 + 1
        return date(year, month, self.issued_date.day)

    def status(self, as_of: date = None) -> CertificationStatus:
        if as_of is None:
            as_of = date.today()
        if self.suspended:
            return CertificationStatus.SUSPENDED
        if as_of > self.expiry_date:
            return CertificationStatus.EXPIRED
        return CertificationStatus.CERTIFIED

    def days_until_expiry(self, as_of: date = None) -> int:
        if as_of is None:
            as_of = date.today()
        return (self.expiry_date - as_of).days

    def renewal_due_warning(self, warning_days: int = 60) -> bool:
        return 0 < self.days_until_expiry() <= warning_days


@dataclass
class CertificationRegistry:
    certs: list[Certification] = field(default_factory=list)

    def active_for_role(self, holder_id: str, required_cert_name: str) -> bool:
        for cert in self.certs:
            if (cert.holder_id == holder_id
                    and cert.cert_name == required_cert_name
                    and cert.status() == CertificationStatus.CERTIFIED):
                return True
        return False

    def expiring_soon(self, within_days: int = 60) -> list[Certification]:
        return [c for c in self.certs if c.renewal_due_warning(within_days)]

    def uncertified_users(self, required_cert: str, user_ids: list[str]) -> list[str]:
        return [
            uid for uid in user_ids
            if not self.active_for_role(uid, required_cert)
        ]


# Build a small registry
registry = CertificationRegistry(certs=[
    Certification(
        cert_id="CERT-2026-0041",
        cert_name="AI Knowledge Worker",
        track="B",
        issued_date=date(2025, 7, 1),
        validity_months=12,
        renewal_requirements=[
            "Complete any curriculum updates issued since last certification",
            "Pass the annual hallucination detection lab (score >= 75%)",
        ],
        issuing_organisation="Enterprise AI Programme",
        holder_id="EMP-001",
    ),
    Certification(
        cert_id="CERT-2026-0088",
        cert_name="AI Knowledge Worker",
        track="B",
        issued_date=date(2026, 5, 15),
        validity_months=12,
        renewal_requirements=[
            "Complete any curriculum updates issued since last certification",
            "Pass the annual hallucination detection lab (score >= 75%)",
        ],
        issuing_organisation="Enterprise AI Programme",
        holder_id="EMP-004",
    ),
])

check_date = date(2026, 7, 1)
print(f"Registry status as of {check_date}:")
for cert in registry.certs:
    status = cert.status(check_date)
    days = cert.days_until_expiry(check_date)
    print(f"  {cert.holder_id} -- {cert.cert_name} -- {status.value} ({days} days to expiry)")

expiring = registry.expiring_soon(within_days=60)
print(f"\nExpiring within 60 days: {len(expiring)}")
for c in expiring:
    print(f"  {c.holder_id}: expires {c.expiry_date}")

required_users = ["EMP-001", "EMP-002", "EMP-003", "EMP-004"]
uncertified = registry.uncertified_users("AI Knowledge Worker", required_users)
print(f"\nUncertified users (of {len(required_users)} required): {uncertified}")
Lab Environment Design

Lab scenarios require a controlled environment: a sandboxed instance of the AI tool running on synthetic or anonymised data, an assessment interface for recording findings, and a scoring system that can evaluate detection accuracy against the ground truth. The three common approaches each have trade-offs:
Lab Environment Type
Setup Cost
Realism
Scoring Automation
Best For
Sandboxed production tool
Medium -- requires IT provisioning of a training tenant
High -- exact tool the learner will use
Manual -- assessor reviews submissions
Practitioner and Advanced tracks where tool fidelity matters
Static scenario with pre-generated outputs
Low -- scenario authored once, reused many times
Medium -- output is realistic but not live-generated
High -- ground truth is fixed; can automate scoring
Track A and B foundation-level detection labs
Live tool with synthetic data
High -- requires synthetic data pipeline and live environment
Very high -- model behaviour is authentic
Low -- outputs vary; human review required
Track C builders who need to observe model behaviour under variation
Renewal and Recertification

Renewal is not the same as initial certification. An employee who was certified 11 months ago has 11 months of tool experience; their renewal does not need to repeat the full curriculum. Renewal should target the delta: what has changed since the last certification that requires verified competence update?
Annual hallucination detection lab re-sit.
The core practical skill degrades without reinforcement. An annual lab with a new scenario (not the same one from initial certification) maintains the habit of systematic validation.
Curriculum delta module.
If the curriculum was updated since the last certification (new tool version, new known limitations, policy change), the employee must complete only the updated module, not the entire curriculum.
Incident-triggered early renewal.

If the employee was involved in a P1 or P2 incident as a user or reviewer, early recertification may be required. This is not punitive -- it verifies that the employee has updated their working model of the tool's failure modes.
Certification as a Go-Live Gate

For roles where an AI error in production would cause client harm, financial loss, or regulatory exposure, certification should be a hard gate on tool access, not a soft expectation. This is operationalised through the certification registry: the tool's access control layer checks certification status before granting access to the production environment.
Gate Type
Mechanism
Appropriate For
Override Process
Hard gate -- tool access blocked
Certification registry API checked at login; uncertified users cannot access the tool
High-risk roles: legal output, financial advice, medical summary, customer-facing output
Programme Manager approval + audit log entry; limited to 5 business days
Soft gate -- manager notification
Certification registry sends alert to line manager and OCM team when user is uncertified
Medium-risk roles: internal use, reviewed before delivery, non-client-facing
Manager acknowledges and accepts responsibility; no tool restriction
Monitoring only
Certification status recorded in audit log alongside output record
Low-risk roles: AI-assisted drafts, always human-reviewed before use
No override needed -- uncertified use is logged but not blocked
Common Lab and Certification Design Failures
Lab uses the same scenario for assessment that was used in training.
Learners memorise the planted errors rather than developing detection skill. Use distinct scenario sets for training practice and assessment.
Certification is permanent with no expiry.
Tool updates, policy changes, and new failure modes render a permanent certification meaningless within months. All certifications must have expiry dates and renewal requirements.
Lab errors are too obvious.

Planting an error that reads '£500 billion' in a £50,000 contract tests whether learners are awake, not whether they can validate. Planted errors should require genuine application of the validation checklist to detect.
No link between certification and tool access.
A certification programme that has no consequence for non-completion produces low completion rates in roles where time pressure is highest -- exactly the roles where certification matters most.
Renewal is designed as a full restart.
Requiring experienced employees to repeat the entire foundation curriculum at renewal treats all updates as equivalent. Target renewal at the delta; respect the learner's existing competence.
Summary

Hands-on labs and certification paths bridge the gap between declarative knowledge and procedural skill. Three certification tracks -- User Proficiency (Track A), Knowledge Worker (Track B), and Builder/Engineer (Track C) -- define structured paths with lab-based assessment culminating in time-limited credentials. Lab scenarios must use realistic inputs, contain planted errors discoverable through the taught validation process, and require documentation of findings. Certification registries track issue dates, expiry, and renewal triggers; for high-risk roles, certification status gates tool access in production. Renewal targets the competence delta since last certification -- an annual lab re-sit and curriculum delta module -- not a full restart. The most important design principle: a certification that tests recall rather than detection skill certifies knowledge, not the ability to use AI safely in the role.
